sources.notes: Official editorial confirms reverse propagation of winning/losing states as vertices are updated.
Independent writeup used to cross-check the dynamic DAG game DP interpretation.

annotation.summary: Maintain game outcomes on a directed acyclic graph while vertices change state monotonically.
annotation.constraints: The graph and updates are large, so each edge/vertex should be processed only when its game state actually changes.
annotation.core_idea: Game states on a DAG are determined by successor states. Since updates are monotone, a losing/winning change can be propagated backward with counters: when all outgoing options become bad or at least one good option appears, predecessor states are updated and pushed into a queue.
tricks: Use reverse edges for game-state propagation.
Maintain counts of currently winning/losing successors.
Exploit monotone updates so each state transition is processed once.

solution_variants.name: reverse-dag-game-propagation
solution_variants.summary: Compute and maintain DP game states on a DAG using reverse adjacency and counters under monotone updates.

tags.evidence: Each vertex outcome is a normal-play winning/losing state derived from successor outcomes.
The solution maintains dynamic DP states over vertices based on their outgoing neighbors.
Acyclicity makes reverse topological/game-state propagation valid.
The state transitions are propagated along directed edges.
Changed states are processed through a propagation queue.
